note_data.title: Introduction
keyPoints: Physics is the branch of science which deals with the study of matter, energy and their mutual relationship
Physical quantities are quantities which can be measured and have numerical values with proper units
Base quantities are fundamental quantities which cannot be expressed in terms of other quantities
Derived quantities are quantities which can be expressed in terms of base quantities
SI system has seven base units: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela
Significant figures are meaningful digits in a measured or calculated value
Scientific notation is used to express very large or very small numbers conveniently
Prefixes like micro, milli, kilo, mega are used to express multiples and sub-multiples of units

definitions.definition: Physics is the branch of science which deals with the study of matter, energy and their mutual relationship.
A physical quantity is a quantity which can be measured and has a numerical value with proper unit.
Base quantities are fundamental quantities which cannot be expressed in terms of other physical quantities.
Derived quantities are quantities which can be expressed in terms of base quantities.
Unit is the standard amount of a physical quantity chosen to measure the physical quantity of the same kind.

importantQuestions.question: Define physics and give two examples of physical quantities.
importantQuestions.answer: Physics is the branch of science which deals with the study of matter, energy and their mutual relationship. Examples: length, mass, time, force, velocity.

importantQuestions.question: Differentiate between base quantities and derived quantities with examples. Also write their SI units.
importantQuestions.answer: Base quantities are fundamental quantities which cannot be expressed in terms of other quantities. Examples: length (meter), mass (kilogram), time (second). Derived quantities can be expressed in terms of base quantities. Examples: area (m²), velocity (m/s), force (kg⋅m/s²), density (kg/m³).

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a base quantity?
The SI unit of electric current is:
How many significant figures are in 0.00456?

commonMistakes: Students confuse base quantities with derived quantities
Writing wrong SI units for physical quantities
Incorrect counting of significant figures, especially with zeros
Forgetting to write units in final answers
Mixing up prefixes and their numerical values (kilo = 10³, not 10²)
examTips: This chapter usually has 2-3 MCQs and 1 short question about definitions
Memorize all 7 base quantities and their SI units perfectly
Practice significant figures counting - very common in MCQs
Learn prefixes table by heart (micro to tera)
Always define terms exactly as written in STBB textbook
formulas: Prefixes: micro(10⁻⁶), milli(10⁻³), centi(10⁻²), kilo(10³), mega(10⁶)
note_data.keyDiagram: Table showing 7 base quantities (Length, Mass, Time, Electric Current, Temperature, Amount of Substance, Luminous Intensity) with their symbols and SI units. This table appears in every BSEK paper.
